quotedTweet.text:
So we keep talking about Ukrainian drones attacking Russian refineries. 

Pulling out my Refinery Configuration Schematic, I see 3 big scenarios:

1⃣If the drones struck the CDU (the crosses in blue), there's refinery downtime and the immediate hit is on distillates production and more crude exports.

2⃣If the drones struck the reformer (the small yellow crosses), you are going to get less mogas but more naphtha exports from Russia. 

3⃣If the drones struck the important secondary units like the FCC and HCU (the big red crosses), you are going to get less mogas and diesel respectively, and more fuel oil exports from Russia (if the CDU is still running). 

Also it usually takes a longer time to repair damaged secondary units vs the CDU due to more sophisticated parts.

I don't have visibility of where the strikes happened, but we can see European product cracks for direction.
 
#oott

HEDGE FUNDS and other money managers are much more bullish about the outlook for the price of gasoline than diesel or crude oil. U.S. gasoline inventories were severely depleted during the war between the United States and Iran as refiners prioritised production of more jet fuel. Fund managers have boosted their net position in U.S. gasoline futures and options to a 14-week high equivalent to 72 million barrels (78th percentile for all weeks since 2011). The net position is far more bullish than for diesel (52 million barrels, 43rd percentile) or crude (141 million barrels, 1st percentile):

text:
Either Iran controls the Strait of Hormuz or it doesn’t. There’s no in-between.

https://t.co/T1GriM7IHH
The Iran route is getting all the traffic flow. https://t.co/q2BgiwsJUW

Iran fired a missile at a Qatari ship which is really sending a message, “Where’s my money?”

But the US knows the moment Qatar releases the money to Iran, IRGC will just close the Oman lane anyways. 

🤷‍♂️

Strait of Hormuz over the last 24 hours.

As expected, the Oman route will be heavily contested in the coming days. The inbound tanker flows have slowed to a trickle and with the latest attack from IRGC using missiles (previously drones), the situation appears to be escalating.

From a flow standpoint, inbound tanker rate is insufficient to change the production shut-in math. Yes, there are some loadings going on, but if the onshore storage drains with no visibility for a lot more VLCCs, the production will not restart, which is the crux of the issue.

I guess time will tell what happens from here on out. I suspect the US will keep testing Iran's resolve with the Oman route.

Note: At the beginning of the video, you will see the large group of vessels leaving associated with Japan. 

I have included video from @MarineTraffic and my table of inbound tankers below.

text:
A buddy I trust told me, "Iran should know it can’t get control over Hormuz with 1-2 missiles attack."

I'm 100% aligned on that. Tehran seems to be trying to flex and claw back control over the strait since the last long weekend, but I seriously question their stomach for real risk here.

For starters, the US pulling the sanctions waiver is pretty much a nothingburger. That 60 day exemption clock was already ticking down to August 21st anyway.

Most refiners had already locked in their August arrival slates, and a 60 day window is way too tight for legal teams to clear internal compliance hurdles and shipping logistics.

Since the bulk of that Iranian flow was front-running straight to China regardless of the waiver, today's headline does zip to the physical balances. Unless Washington rolls out a hard naval blockade, nothing really changes.

But look, everyone on the street knows Trump's default setting is the TACO trade, and I don't see him pulling off any hardline measures. Even if he tries to flex, he'll just TACO right back out.

That leaves Iran as the only player left to force a tail risk scenario, but do they actually want to jump off that cliff? 

The data doesn't lie—as the charts show, they basically sat on their hands and let the barrels clear even while the oil was steadily flowing.

Everyone can probably agree we were well overdue for a tactical bounce anyway, but the reality is that it feels nowhere near enough to fundamentally shift the broader picture.

Are they really going to push past a simple show of strength for negotiating leverage? Hmmm.... 

#oott #iran

Just wrap your head around what’s played out over the last two months—it's been a non-stop circus of absolute insanity.

And look at the tape today. You get multiple tankers taking fire, plus the headline of the waiver cancellation—even if it's got zero teeth—and the flat price prints a measly $3.5 bounce.

If you want a pure, unadulterated look at just how deeply skepticism is baked into this market, that's your textbook example. The street is literally acting like it won't price in a premium unless a nuke actually drops on the Middle East.

Look, even if I think the market's reaction is completely detached from reality, playing it close to the chest here is a perfectly rational stance. I have no idea why some guys are catching such a massive feelings hurt over this.

Global commodities CTA positioning.

Definitely seeing highly overcrowded spaces right now. The trade is just begging for a clean reversal catalyst to wake up the tape.

Trump says Greenland should be controlled by the U.S., calling it strategically vital for national security.

Trump acknowledged such a move would damage U.S. ties with NATO.  

Trump argued Denmark has failed to invest sufficiently in Greenland and said the island is becoming increasingly important as China and Russia expand their Arctic presence.

Arabica #coffee futures surged as much as 19% on Monday, the steepest one-day rise in 26 years, as El Niño-related weather concerns extended a one-month rally that has lifted prices by 45%. Widespread expectations of a bumper crop in top producer Brazil have been tempered by poor weather that has delayed harvesting, prompting some growers to hold back sales in anticipation of higher prices. Meanwhile, #cocoa futures jumped 13% to their highest since January as persistent rains across key West African growing regions added to supply concerns, squeezing funds holding sizeable short positions.
